event: Abolition of the tax on Hindu pilgrims
situation: Akbar faced the challenge of governing a religiously diverse population while seeking political stability.
facts: A tax was imposed on Hindu pilgrims visiting sacred sites.
The tax was unpopular among Hindu subjects.
The Mughal state relied on revenue from various taxes.
choices: Abolish the pilgrim tax to promote religious harmony.
Retain the tax to preserve traditional revenue sources.
learning_focus: Evaluating religious tolerance as a strategy for social cohesion and legitimacy.

event: Abolition of the Jizya tax on non-Muslims
situation: Akbar aimed to strengthen imperial unity while ruling over a religiously diverse population.
facts: Jizya was a tax levied on non-Muslim subjects.
The tax symbolized religious distinction in governance.
Many officials and orthodox groups supported its continuation.
choices: Abolish the Jizya to promote equality among subjects.
Retain the Jizya to uphold traditional Islamic fiscal policy.
learning_focus: Assessing the political impact of inclusive policies in a multi-religious empire.

situation: Akbar faced an internal conspiracy led by powerful nobles seeking to depose him.
facts: The Uzbeg nobles held high positions in the Mughal court.
The revolt aimed to replace Akbar as emperor.
Rebellion threatened central authority and stability.
choices: Crush the revolt decisively to assert imperial authority.
Attempt reconciliation with rebel nobles to avoid further unrest.
learning_focus: Managing internal power struggles and maintaining centralized control.
source: Akbarnama and accounts of the Uzbeg Revolt, 1567

event: Capture of Chittorgarh Fort after prolonged siege
situation: Akbar confronted strong Rajput resistance centered on the fortified stronghold of Chittorgarh.
facts: Chittorgarh was a major Rajput fort in Mewar.
The fort was considered difficult to capture.
The siege resulted in heavy casualties.
choices: Continue the siege until total military victory is achieved.
Lift the siege and seek a negotiated settlement with Mewar.
learning_focus: Understanding the role of decisive military action in territorial expansion.

situation: Akbar aimed to eliminate the last major center of Rajput independence in Rajasthan to secure imperial dominance.
facts: Ranthambore was one of the strongest forts in Rajasthan.
It was held by Rajput rulers resisting Mughal authority.
Its surrender marked the end of large-scale Rajput military resistance.
choices: Accept the surrender and integrate the region into the empire.
Punish the defenders harshly to deter future rebellions.
learning_focus: Balancing military victory with political integration after conquest.

event: Birth of Prince Salim, Akbar’s heir
situation: After years without a surviving heir, Akbar sought stability for the future of the Mughal dynasty.
facts: Prince Salim was born in 1569.
Akbar associated the birth with the blessings of Salim Chishti.
The birth secured dynastic succession.
choices: Publicly honor the saint and promote spiritual patronage.
Treat the birth as a royal matter without religious association.
learning_focus: Understanding the role of succession and spiritual belief in imperial legitimacy.

event: Foundation of Fatehpur Sikri as a new imperial capital
situation: Akbar aimed to project imperial authority and promote unity through architecture and urban planning.
facts: Fatehpur Sikri was built near the shrine of Salim Chishti.
The city combined Persian, Islamic, and Indian architectural styles.
It served as the Mughal capital for several years.
choices: Establish Fatehpur Sikri as the primary imperial capital.
Continue ruling from Agra and limit Fatehpur Sikri to a ceremonial role.
learning_focus: Exploring how architecture and city planning reinforce political ideology.

situation: Akbar moved swiftly to prevent rebellion in Gujarat and secure control over critical maritime trade routes.
facts: Gujarat was a wealthy region with major ports.
Akbar covered approximately 600 miles in about 11 days.
Control of Gujarat strengthened Mughal trade and revenue.
choices: Launch a rapid military campaign to assert direct control.
Delay action and negotiate with local rulers and merchants.
learning_focus: Evaluating speed and decisiveness in military strategy and economic expansion.
